rhetorical_signals: But if we take a broad view of the field, some remaining obstacles emerge.
the compound does not have oral bioavailability, which we need to advance this compound into the clinic.

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Reliance on charged residues prevents passive membrane permeation, limiting oral bioavailability.

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker highlighted that many macrocycles still lack intrinsic oral bioavailability and require formulation tricks, indicating a chemistry limitation.
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Pursuing oral indication while current leads remain injectable; risk of diluting clinical signal with formulation-dependent exposure.

key_data_gaps: No oral bioavailability data in humans
No human PK/PD or safety data

crispro_opportunity.description: Identify patient subsets with high cyclin A/B activity and low plasma protein binding to prioritize for oral macrocycle development.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted that CID078 shows potent inhibition in small cell lung cancer lines with specific genetic dysregulations but lacks oral exposure.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model macrocycle permeability and predict plasma protein binding to guide design of orally bioavailable variants.

crispro_opportunity.description: Rescue the CID078 series by computationally redesigning the charged arginine pharmacophore to improve passive permeability.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker described the charged arginine residue as a barrier to membrane permeation.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can explore alternative side‑chain chemistries that retain binding while reducing charge.

cited_competitors.name: Merck PCSK9 inhibitors
cited_competitors.context: Cited as an example of an extracellular target that achieved oral dosing despite charge considerations.

cited_competitors.name: IL‑23 oral inhibitor
cited_competitors.context: Mentioned as a successful orally bioavailable extracellular modality.

cited_competitors.name: Boehringer Ingelheim
cited_competitors.context: Collaboration on an undisclosed family of cyclin inhibitors.

unresolved_questions: What specific chemical modifications will enable true oral bioavailability for CID078?
Can the macrocycle scaffold be scaled manufacturably at commercial scale?

rhetorical_signals: we have about 24 hours exposure above the free cell cellular IC50.
the body weights of the highest dose were actually more than 10%, and this proved an early indication of a narrow therapeutic index.

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Off‑target gut toxicity possibly due to weak COX inhibition from ibuprofen‑derived scaffold.
vulnerability_identified.hidden_tox_signal: Speaker noted body‑weight loss >10% at the highest dose indicating dose‑limiting toxicity.

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed that the core scaffold is a known NSAID scaffold, potentially limiting novelty and exposing off‑target COX activity.
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Advancing a scaffold with known COX activity may complicate safety profile and regulatory path.

key_data_gaps: No human pharmacokinetic or efficacy data

crispro_opportunity.description: Use CrisPRO to predict and redesign the scaffold to eliminate COX off‑target activity while retaining DHX8 binding.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker linked gut toxicity to possible COX inhibition from ibuprofen‑derived metabolites.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model off‑target binding to COX enzymes and suggest alternative chemotypes.

crispro_opportunity.description: Leverage the fragment‑based, structure‑guided workflow as a differentiator against competitors lacking DHX8 inhibitors.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker emphasized the novelty of the first DHX8 inhibitors and the structural insights guiding optimization.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can accelerate fragment‑to‑lead transitions for other RNA helicases, expanding the platform.

cited_competitors.name: Akcent Therapeutics (ATX968)
cited_competitors.context: Compared DHX8 inhibitors to allosteric DHX9 inhibitor ATX968, highlighting selectivity differences.

unresolved_questions: Is the observed toxicity on‑target (DHX8 inhibition) or off‑target (COX inhibition)?
Can the therapeutic window be widened without losing potency?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Activation lethality: drugging oncogenic pathways by targeting negative regulators
rhetorical_signals: cancers can't tolerate too much oncogenic activity.
our MAPK activators are much more effective in those resistant populations.

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Potential activation of MAPK signaling in normal tissues could drive unwanted proliferation.
vulnerability_identified.hidden_tox_signal: Speaker noted no observed oncogenesis in normal cells but acknowledged lack of long‑term data.

competitive_moat_weakness.vulnerable_ip_or_chemistry: Novel PPI disruptor (ERK‑p15) and molecular‑glue degrader (CK1α)
competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed structural details of the binding interface, potentially enabling rapid mimicry by competitors.
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Reliance on untested sequencing schedules for cross‑sensitization may dilute clinical signal.

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed selective phospho‑ERK activation in tumor versus normal tissue, then concluded that there is no concern for oncogenesis in healthy tissues – contradictory because activation of MAPK could theoretically drive proliferation in normal cells.

key_data_gaps: No in vivo safety data on normal tissue proliferation
No human efficacy or PK data
Optimal dosing schedule for cross‑sensitization not defined

crispro_opportunity.description: Pair MAPK activators with existing BRAF/MEK inhibitors to exploit cross‑sensitization and overcome resistance.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker demonstrated that MAPK activator treatment resensitized dabrafenib‑resistant tumors to the inhibitor.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model synergistic pathway modulation and predict optimal sequencing regimens.

crispro_opportunity.description: Use CrisPRO to predict emergent resistance mutations to MAPK activators and design next‑generation molecules.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker acknowledged that resistance to activators does arise and can be combated by re‑sensitizing to inhibitors.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate mutational landscapes and suggest scaffold modifications to retain activity.

crispro_opportunity.description: Design adaptive trial enrolling patients with MAPK‑mutant tumors who have progressed on inhibitors, with built‑in crossover to activator therapy.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted the need for intelligent sequencing and cross‑sensitization studies.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate biomarker data to define enrichment criteria for such adaptive designs.

cited_competitors.name: dabrafenib
cited_competitors.context: Used as a MAPK inhibitor to which activator therapy can resensitize resistant tumors.

cited_competitors.name: BRAF inhibitors (general)
cited_competitors.context: Mentioned as standard of care that develops resistance, motivating activator approach.

unresolved_questions: What is the optimal sequencing schedule for activator‑inhibitor combination?
How quickly does resistance to the activator emerge in vivo?
Can activation lethality be applied to loss‑of‑function driven cancers?
